titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.name: University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities
The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive Research and Development Team
University of South Carolina Center for Digital Humanities

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: BEAUTIFUL SANTA BARBARA AN OUT-OF-DOOR PARADISE

title.hi: BEAUTIFUL SANTA BARBARA AN OUT-OF-DOOR PARADISE

div.p: It Is the Riviera of the Great Pacific Coast, and Was Named For a Girl Killed By Her Father—Daughter of a Roman General Who Perished a Christian Martyr—California Found in 1542, and Explored Just Fifty Years After Columbus Discovered America—Santa Barbara Is the Place Where Tired People Come to Soak Up Sunshine—It Is Rich, But Democratic—A Single Grapevine Covers an Acre, Produces Ten Tons of Grapes, Is Eight Feet in Circumference at the Base—Walnuts Yield $200 or More to the Acre.
Please inform us how California compares in size with Pennsylvania?

Ans.—There cannot be a comparison. California has 99,463,681 acres. To get a comparative idea you can take the states of Maine, Vermont, New Hampshire, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, New York, Pennsylvania, New Jersey, Delaware and Maryland, and spreading them all out together over California there would be an uncovered margin of 13,774 square miles, or more than enough in excess to make more than ten states like Rhode Island. California is "some" state.

There is more than one outdoor paradise in California. In the list do not fail to include Santa Barbara. The climate has attracted invalids and persons in search of health and pleasure since the earliest settlement of the Pacific coast. While California was still a part of Mexico, many refined and aristocratic Spanish families made it their home and contributed to the charm of social life. A few descendants of the early Spanish settlers still reside there. They have become thoroughly Americanized, but may easily be identified by their names, their features and complexions and the graceful manners of their race.

The mission of Santa Barbara is the best preserved in California, its monastery is the most extensive, it is an educational institution of high standard, and its influence is the widest of any in the state.

Many people consider Santa Barbara the most desirable place of residence for families of wealth and leisure in the United States, if not in the world. The climate and environment are very much like those of the Riviera of France. It is the only place of resort in the United States which has a mountain range on one side of it, and an ocean on the other, and where the climate permits the population to live in comfort the year around. It is often called the Newport of the Pacific, but is more democratic and cosmopolitan than the queen of the Atlanic resorts. No social tyranny is recognized; the habits and customs of the people are not so artificial; everybody lives as nearly as possible to nature: [sic] there is no rivalry in dress or dinner parties, or other forms of display; all classes spend most of their time out-of-doors, and the belle of Santa Barbara shows to the best advantage in a Khaki suit, astride a mustang climbing a mountain trail, rather than at a ball or garden party. The population is made up mostly of "remitters"—that is in this instance people who have retired from business, widows of large means, and others who are able to live luxuriously upon the proceeds of investments elsewhere. Many people from San Francisco spent part of the winters in Santa Barbara, owning or renting bungalows, which are of all sizes. There are colonies of people from the big eastern cities of Boston and New York. The richest resident is a petroleum prince, who has figured in the divorce courts.

Between the mission and the sea runs the main street of the town, a wide, well-paved thoroughfare, more than two miles long, which is lined on both sides with business blocks, banks, mercantile establishments, and the best bookstores and curio shops to be found on the Pacific coast. The high class of goods they keep is an index to the wealth and culture of the community. There is a line of trolley cars running from the beach to the mission and a wide and beautiful boulevard follows the shore line for several miles. At the northern end are bath houses, and a bathing pier near a plaza, where a band plays every afternoon. There is sea bathing the year round, and one of the finest bath houses in the land. There are several sanitariums and hot medicinal springs at an elevation of 1,300 feet. One may spend an entire winter in Santa Barbara, and follow a different drive or trail every day.

In fact, no city in California has so many beautiful drives and trails for motors, carriages and saddle horses. The Santa Ynez range of mountains is broken by innumerable canyons and gorges, and between them lie broad table lands, which are occupied as ranches, olive groves and walnut orchards. There is a drive or a trail through every canyon, and one of them known as La Cumbre trail, twelve miles long, offers a continuous panorama of glorious scenery. It crosses the mountains through a pass a little less than 4,000 feet above the seas. Another trail reaches the summit of the range by a climb of twelve to fourteen miles, and is equally attractive. The lowest pass is 2,224 feet, but it is possible to climb nearly 8,000 either on horseback or by motor.

In Santa Barbara there is no rivalry in architecture or extravagance. The great competition is in flowers, plants and vegetable gardens. Most of the residences are of the villa type, simple and often severe. The ideal house is of shingles, stained brown, with wide porches hidden by climbing roses and other vines and furnished with hammocks and easy chairs. The fact that the temperature rarely varies more than twelve to fifteen degrees from one year's end to another, and is always moderatley warm, enables everybody to live in the open air, and is, of course, a blessing to the infirm and weary, and has a wholesome influence. People from the east go to Santa Barbara to be soaked in sunshine, and play golf 365 days in the year. The villa population is quite exclusive and has a society of its own. During the winter season the two great hotels, the Potter, probably as large as the largest on the coast, and one of the finest, and the Arlington, which has recently been erected upon the site of an old house of the same name, are filled with eastern people. During the summer middle west and western people take advantage at the hotels of the lower rates. The Potter hotel is very close to the ocean, where the sea bathers revel, and the great lawn on that side is in fact a perfect ocean of flowers.

The Arlington hotel is also attractive. It belongs to E. P. Ripley, president of the Santa Fe railroad, and he is the man who controlled the contracting company which built the Lackawanna railroad from Binghamton to Buffalo about 1880. One of his subcontractors was the late John B. McDonald, who made fame and wealth as the builder of the New York city subway. Mr. Ripley in 1880, was in a way, Mr. McDonald's financial backer. The writer saw Mr. Ripley recently in Santa Barbara, and he looks almost as strong as he did over thirty years ago. His hotel is built of concrete and steel and is after the mission style of architecture, has 300 rooms and cost nearly a half million dollars. The mission idea is carried out in every room and corridor, the woodwork being left in the rough and stained a dark brown by the use of petroleum. There are no ceilings. The heavy rafters are left uncovered; the walls are rough plaster, painted to match the woodwork; the floors are red tiles and even the bath tubs are of mission design.

The mission style of architecture with its strong but simples [sic] lines, [with its ...] Moors and introduced into Mexico, was used by the Franciscan missionaries because they found it the best suited to the climate. That was the reason it was adopted in the south of Spain. The brick and tile did not reflect, but absorbed the glare of the sun. The cloisters are cool at noonday and the thick walls are proof agianst the dampness of the rainy season. It is being used more and more for public buildings, school houses, hotels, and nearly all the new schools and public edifices are being built after the mision design. The government architects planned the conventional granite for the federal post-office at Riverside, Cal., but to the end that a harmonious architectrure should prevail in the city, President Taft directed that the Riverside post-office should conform to the mission style.

All about Santa Barabara is attractive. The entire amphitheater inclosed by the Santa Ynez mountains on the one side and the Pacific ocean on the other, is entirely taken up by rich ranchmen, who own large estates and keep most of their lands in natural parks. They visit in automobiles and on horseback; they wear sombreros and comfortable clothing; they take pride in their crops and orchards and brag about their walnuts and their olives. This is the walnut country par excellence, and it is the best paying crop. The trees require ten years to mature, but give no further trouble. It is only necessary to keep down the weeds and loosen the surface of the soil with plow and harrow. Walnut trees are not subject to diseases like fruit trees. The nuts require no refrigeration, cannot spoil on the hands of the grower, and sell for not less than twelve cents a pound on the cars. The average profit to the grower is $200 an acre. Walnut orchards are mostly limited in area. The largest in California has only 350 acres, and most of them are of less than twenty acres. But the business is extending rapidly.

The walnut acreage will soon be increased when the entire Golita valley is irrigated. Irrigation will enable the farmers to extend the walnut and lemon groves and grow more beans, for these three are great staples. Santa Barbara has been boring a hold through the Santa Ynez range of mountains for a large and permanent water supply. The tunnel is seven by nine feet in size and 7,000 feet through the mountain. Aqueducts were designed for both sides and the aqueducts' supply of water for drinking, irrigation, etc., will be enough for a population of 250,000 people. No one dreams that Santa Barbara will ever have such a population. The new scheme is to irrigate the Golita valley.

Writing of lemons, it is worth mention that the Crocker family have an enormous lemon ranch in the Montecito valley, just over a ridge from Santa Barbara, with a large stone packing house and all the appurtenances and ship their crops by the train load. It is the most extensive and complete lemon ranch in America, with 2,000 acres of trees. The crops are truly wonderful. In this temperature, and in a soil of surpassing richness, everything grows. All the plants known to the temperature and semi-temperate regions live happily together. The palm and the pine, the apple and the oleander[,] the maple and the magnolia grow side by side with equal vigor. Roses bloom and strawberry beds give ripened fruit every month. The cultivation of vegetables and small fruits for the local market, is the most profitable kind of farming, however, as hotels and boarding houses consume large quantities of both. The cultivation of flowers and plants is also a profitable business. On the main street of Santa Barbara is a concern which imports shrubs, vines, plants and trees from everywhere and acclimates them for California. You can see nearly a half hundred kinds of palms, camphor and cinnamon trees, papyrus, bamboos of all sorts and sizes, hibiscus, agavas, bread-fruit, and other strange plants that you read about in Robinson Crusoe, the Swiss Family Robinson and other tales of the South Sea islands.

Out in Montecito park an oil and steel magnate named Gillespie has a great show place. It took eight years to construct the palace and surroundings at a cost of more than $3,000,000. It is first of all a palm park. But it is more. The home is copied after some Roman villa, and the grounds likewise. This palace and palm park is open to visitors at certain hours each day when Mr. Gillespie is away. The house has a great plunge like to the Roman baths. The water runs into high reservoirs, which as they are located along the descending hillside in front are tapped by pipes from which the palms are watered. Mr. Gillespie has been traveling the world over for many years looking for new varieties of palms[.] He now has about eighty distinct varieties and has sold single plants for as much as $500. A Buffalo man, the head of the Pierce-Arrow Automobile company, occupies the palace this winter.

Some of the finest grapes in California are grown about Santa Barbara the vines having been imported from Spain in the early days by the Franciscan fathers who taught the natives how to make wine as well as olive oil. The largest grape vine in the world, it is said, grows in Carpinteria valley, a few miles from Santa Barbara. It was planted in 1843 by a Spanish farmer and has yielded ten tons of grapes in a single season. It spreads over an acre of ground and is supported by seventy-eight posts. If it were not pruned so closely every season it would cover a much larger area. Six inches from the ground the trunk measures eight and one-half feet in circumference, and where it reaches the trellis and begins to spread, about six and one-half feet from the ground, the girth is five feet seven inches. There are several other very large vines int he same neighborhood. One of the largest ever known was cut down and sent to the Philadelphia centennial exposition in 1876, as a sample of California products and was a great advertisment for the state.

It will be of interest to all who love the words and music of the song "The Rosary," to know the words were written in Santa Barbara by Cameron Rogers. He is an artist; he lives there and from his studio window there is a most entrancing view. It was set to music by Ethelbert Nevin. Nearby to the scenes of beauty in Santa Barbara one can find the greasy conditions of a rich oil country. Crude petroleum gushes up from the earth and sea at Summerland, a town near Santa Barbara. It is occupied by a colony of Spiritualists. It looks like one of the old fashioned oil towns of Pennsylvania. The first petroleum was discovered on the hillside, and the veins were followed down to the ocean, where what the Californians call "submarine wells" have been bo[r]ed and piped and are now flowing regularly. The oil comes up first through the sand, and then piped through the water and thence to the reservoirs on shore. The people of Summerland were the first to discover the adaptability of crude petroleum for making roads. It was an accident. The first man to use the crude petroleum on a road was so lazy that he shoveled the refuse from an oil tank into the street to get rid of it, when he should have carted it off to some dumping ground. The oil immediately absorbed the dust, and the heavy metal wheels which passed over it packed it down into a hard, smooth surface like asphaltum which shed the rains and was never muddy or dusty. His neighbors then put oil on the roadways in front of their houses to keep down the dust, and the practice finally became general and is now used effectively in every part of the country[.]

The history of Santa Barbara is very romantic, but the town acquired its name from a tragedy, due to a father's killing of his daughter, and after her it was named. Santa Barbara was one of the early martyrs. She was born in Asia Minor, and was the daughter of a Roman general. She was converted to Christianity when she was sixteen years old, in the year A. D. 233, during the reign of Alexander Severus, an emperor frindly to the Christians. His successor, Maximinus, was a thorough pagan and ordered all Christian converts put to death. The father of Barbara was Deoscorus, and he concealed her, hoping to persuade her to recant. When he was unable to do so he inflicted upon her cruel torments, which she bore with wonderful fortitude for a girl of seventeen, and he finally slew her with his own hand. Her piety, her steadfastness for the faith, and her example had great influence with the young Christians and caused her to be canonized by both the Greek and Roman Catholic churches. She suffered martyrdom at Nicomedia, Asia Minor about 235 A. D. It was not by a monk that Santa Barbara was named but by a Portuguese explorer, as will later apear.

Off the coast of Santa Barbara is an archipelago of mountainous islands, the largest being called San Miguel, Santa Rosa and Santa Cruz. When the Spaniards first discovered the country they were all inhabited, but after the mission was established the island families were persuaded by the friars to move over to the mainland and settle near by. Juan Rodnguez [Rodríguez] Cabrillo, the discoverer of California, is buried upon the island of San Miguel, although no trace of his grave remains. He was the first European who is known to have explored the Pacific coast of what is now the United States. He was a navigator of enterprise, courage and tact, and although a Portuguese by birth, spent most of his life in the service of Spain like Columbus and Americus Vespucci, who were Italians.

About the middle of October 1542, just fifty years after Columbus discovered America, Cabrillo landed on the beach of what is now Santa Barbara, and was met by natives in their canoes, who received him with demonstrations of curiosity and kindness, and exchanged presents with him. He remained some time, exploring the surrounding country, which in his reports to the viceroy of Mexico was described as of great attractiveness and fertility. He crossed to the islands of Santa Rosa, Santa Cruz and San Miguel and made the latter his headquarters, while he continued his explorations northward to a point many leagues north of the bay of San Francisco, although he never found the Golden Gate. It is difficult to determine how far north he went, but we know from his narratives that he was driven back by bad weather and returned to San Miguel to recover from a fracture of his right arm, but he grew worse instead of better and on January 8, 1543, he died and was buried on that island.

Santa Barbara was given its name by Sebastian Viscaino, another Portuguese epxlorer, who set sail from Acapulco with three large caravels and a transport, carrying 200 soldiers and three Carmelite friars. Antonio de la Ascension, one of the friars, took copious notes of all they saw and heard along the coast, made a map of considerable detail and as directed by the explorer named each bay, cape, island and other conspicuous landmark after the particular saint whose festival occurred nearest to the day of discovery. On December 4, 1602, the party landed at Santa Barbara, and that being the anniversary of Santa Barbara, her name was given to the bay and the country that surrounds it.

Although Viscaino was not the first discoverer of California, his voyage was the most important because of the maps of the coast and the authentic information which Padre Antonio and the other friars were able to furnish Europe in regard to that part of the world. The island of Santa Cruz recieved its name from the fact that one of the early explorers lost there a crucifix of great sanctity, which was found by the natives and restored as the vessel was about to sail. A romantic narrative relates how in 18[5]1 George Nidever, a ranchero, of Santa Barbara, found human footprints upon the sandy beach of the island of St. Nichalas, close to Santa Cruz, which was supposed to be uninhabited. He was reminded of a story that had been widely circulated about an Indian woman who had been abandoned there and made a thorough search. On the first day he wa unsuccessful; on the second day he found evidences of human existence, including a basket rudely made of reeds; on the [third] day he found three small huts [made] of the ribs of a whale and cove[red] with brush. In one of these huts [was] an Indian woman, with long hair fa[ll]ing over her bare shoulders, and we[ar]ing a single garmet made of [the] skins of birds. She showed no ala[rm] but when Mr. Nidever, and his co[m]panions, approached she offered th[em] roots and a piece of blubber to [eat.] She had entirely lost the faculty [of] speech. When Mr. Nidever told [her] that she was to accompany him to [the] mainland[,] she understood read[ily,] showed much pleasure and gather[ed] together her little belongings, wh[ich] consisted of baskets woven of gra[ss,] jars filled with pebbles and other [little] mementoes. Upon reaching Sa[nta] Barbara Mr. Nidever took her to [his] home, where she was kindly cared [for] until her death three or four ye[ars] later. Although she was never able [to] express herself so as to be under[stood,] she had not forgotten the art of ho[use]keeping, and performed the duties [of] a servant as long as her health p[er]mitted.

Investigation developed the [...] that in the year 1835 the padres [sent] a schooner to the islands to collect [all] the Indians that remained there [and] take them over to the missions. Wh[en] they were ready to depart one of [the] women could not find her child, [and] ran from the boat into the woods [...] search for her. Before her return [a] violent storm arose, and as there [was] no harbor and the anchorage was [...] secure, it became necessary to [...] out into the open sea[.] The cap[tain] intended to return for the woman, [...] for several days the ship was dr[iven] southward by the violence of the [gale] and finally landed its passengers [...] San Pedro, now the port of [Los] Angeles. A few days later the [cap]tain started northward with a [...] of lumber for Monterey, intending [to] stop at the island and take on [the] woman, but another gale capsized [the] schooner and all hands were lo[st. So] the woman was forgotten and re[main]ed for sixteen years alone, like Ro[bin]son Crusoe. It was believed that [the] child died shortly after the deser[tion,] from exposure to the weather.

Until the discovery of gold in [Cal]ifornia, and the rapid development [of] San Francisco, Sacramento and o[ther] places farther north Santa Bar[bara] was second only in importance [to] Monterey, on the coast. The terr[...] occupied by the missionaries [was] divided into four districts, in each [...] which was a presido, or military [p]rison, and the officers exercised [...] civil and military jurisdiction. [Mon]terey was the official seat of the [gov]ernor, and the commandant at S[anta] Barbara was second in author[ity.] Thus it happend that after the d[eath] of Governor Arrellago, in 182[5, ...] Captain Arguello, commandant [of] Santa Barbara, exercised authority [un]til a new governor arrived. After [the] Mexican war Arguello remained [at] Santa Barbara, and became one of [the] most influential men of Cal[ifornia,] where he resided for thirty-four y[ears.] Another important commander [...] Santa Barbara was Lieutenant [Rom]mualdo Pacheco, and of a promin[ent] family. He left a son, Romualdo, [who] became governor of the state, and [a] member of the house of repre[senta]tives. His family still reside in [the] neighborhood.

At the time of the arrival of Ca[ptain] John C. Fremont and his little [...] in 1846, Lieutenant Raimundo Ca[...] was commander at the presidio, [and] the misssion and the property [which] surrounded it were held by Nichola[s ...] Den and Daniel Hill, two Ameri[cans] under a lease from the Mexican [... gov]ernment for a yearly rental of $1,[...] The lease covered all the lands [...] buildings of the missionary except [the] church and the monastery. Some li[...] head of cattle, 1,700 head of sheep [...] many horses were also included. [The] bishop of the California diocese [en]deavored in vain to resist the a[...] of the government, and his death [was] hastened by his failure. He died [in] 1846 and is buried in the Mi[ssion] church.

Daniel Hill, the lessee, was a [Cape] Cod sea captain who first visited [the] coast in command of a trading ve[ssel] in 1822, en route to the Haw[aiian] Islands, and was so much taken [with] the climate and surroundings of S[anta] Barbara, that he brought his [wife] and family and settled there in 1[...] starting a trading store, bought [...] and cattle and became the richest [man] in the community. Nicholas A. [...] an Irish physician, married H[ill's] daughter. He arrived in 1836 and [prac]ticed medicine while he raised ca[ttle.] Before his death he, too, had be[come] very rich and was the owner of [...] large ranches as well as 10,000 ca[ttle.]

Several of the residences of [the] Spanish and Mexican colonial p[...] are still standing, although they [have] been considerably altered on mo[...] lines. The best description of life [in] Santa Barbara at that period may [be] found in Dana's "Two Years Be[fore] the Mast," which in the days of [...] youth was a universally popular [...]
(To Be Continued.)
